notes: Pass rates are identical (96.3% both). The only failed assertion (web search for mortgage rates) failed in BOTH configurations because web search was denied to subagents. This is an environment limitation, not a skill quality issue.
The skill version is significantly more expensive: +50% more tokens (56k vs 38k) and +50% slower (415s vs 278s). This is expected — the skill reads vault notes and produces more detailed output.

Qualitative differences are substantial despite identical pass rates. With-skill outputs: (1) read vault notes for real financial data, (2) consistently applied the freedom ladder framework, (3) followed the structured output format, (4) cross-referenced OKRs and demerger strategy, (5) introduced novel concepts like 'bridge portfolio'.

The baseline produced surprisingly good output — Claude already has strong financial analysis capabilities. The skill's value is in CONSISTENCY (always checks FI impact), PERSONALIZATION (vault data), and FRAMEWORK (freedom ladder, 5-stage pipeline).

Assertion design note: current assertions test for presence of analytical elements (bull/bear, tax rules, etc.) but not for QUALITY or PERSONALIZATION. Adding assertions that test vault data integration would better differentiate skill from baseline.
The failed assertion (web search) cannot be resolved in the eval environment. Consider marking web-search-dependent assertions as 'environment-dependent' in future iterations.
Eval 2 with-skill was notably slower (529s vs 220s baseline). It read 3 vault notes and produced a longer analysis with the ASK tax appendix. The extra time was productive, not wasted.
